nodes.description: Upturned nostrils.

evidence.reference_title: Prenatal-onset INPPL1-related skeletal dysplasia in two unrelated families: Diagnosis and prediction of lethality.
evidence.snippet: Examination of the newborn (case A) shows short limbs with small trident hands and feet, and craniofacial dysmorphism including hypertelorism, depressed nasal bridge, short nose with anteverted nares and depressed nasal bridge, long philtrum, and low set deformed ears.

nodes.description: Bowed long bones, particularly of the lower extremities.

evidence.reference_title: Novel compound heterozygous mutations in inositol polyphosphate phosphatase-like 1 in a family with severe opsismodysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Radiographs of fetus #2 showed hydropic changes and short limbs with flaring and metaphyseal cupping were reported. Both distal femoral metaphyses appeared irregular and the radii, femora and tibae were bowed (Figure 1).

nodes.description: SHIP2 loss disrupts phosphoinositide-dependent cell migration and adhesion signaling. Patient-derived fibroblasts show markedly reduced cell migration and altered adhesion properties, suggesting that these cellular defects contribute to impaired endochondral ossification by disrupting the normal movement and organization of chondrocytes in the growth plate.

evidence.reference_title: Fibroblasts derived from patients with opsismodysplasia display SHIP2-specific cell migration and adhesion defects.
evidence.snippet: cell migration is very much decreased in fibroblasts derived from three OPS patients as compared with control individuals.

evidence.reference_title: Fibroblasts derived from patients with opsismodysplasia display SHIP2-specific cell migration and adhesion defects.
evidence.snippet: signaling events linked to migration and particularly to adhesion, which are lost in OPS patients, would prevent normal endochondral ossification.

nodes.description: Delayed mineralization and appearance of secondary ossification centers is a defining radiographic feature.

evidence.reference_title: Opsismodysplasia: Phosphate Wasting Osteodystrophy Responds to Bisphosphonate Therapy.
evidence.snippet: The main radiological features are severe platyspondyly, short long bones including squared metacarpals, delayed epiphyseal ossification, and metaphyseal flaring and cupping (7–10).

nodes.description: Markedly delayed bone age with retarded ossification of epiphyses is a hallmark radiographic finding.

evidence.reference_title: Opsismodysplasia: a new type of chondrodysplasia with predominant involvement of the bones of the hand and the vertebrae.
evidence.snippet: very retarded bone maturation; marked shortness of the bones of the hands and of the feet with concave metaphyses; and thin, lamellar vertebral bodies.

nodes.description: Flattened nasal bridge.

evidence.reference_title: A Case of Opsismodysplasia with a Novel INPPL1 Variant.
evidence.snippet: He had dysmorphic findings including relative macrocephaly, midface hypoplasia, depressed nasal bridge, anteverted nostrils, long philtrum, small hands and feet, and brachydactyly.

nodes.description: A subset of patients develops renal phosphate wasting with elevated FGF23 levels, leading to hypophosphatemic rickets. Phosphate wasting is associated with more severe bone demineralization and a more severe overall phenotype. The mechanism linking SHIP2 loss to FGF23 elevation and renal phosphate handling is not fully understood.

evidence.reference_title: Hypophosphatemic rickets in opsismodysplasia.
evidence.snippet: We now demonstrate an association between opsismodysplasia, hypophosphatemic rickets, and FGF23 elevation.

evidence.reference_title: Opsismodysplasia: Phosphate Wasting Osteodystrophy Responds to Bisphosphonate Therapy.
evidence.snippet: Renal phosphate wasting is associated with severe bone demineralization and a more severe phenotype.

nodes.description: Pelvic radiographs can show horizontally oriented, dysplastic acetabular roofs.

evidence.reference_title: Novel compound heterozygous mutations in inositol polyphosphate phosphatase-like 1 in a family with severe opsismodysplasia.
evidence.snippet: The iliac bones were decreased in vertical height with horizontal orientation of the acetabular roofs.

nodes.description: Prominent forehead.

evidence.reference_title: Prenatal-onset INPPL1-related skeletal dysplasia in two unrelated families: Diagnosis and prediction of lethality.
evidence.snippet: Opsismodysplasia is an autosomal recessive osteochondrodysplasia disorders characterized by a severe delay in bone maturation. This leads to rhizomelic micromelia with small hands and feet, relative macrocephaly, and craniofacial dysmorphism including frontal bossing, short nose with anteverted nares and depressed nasal bridge, long philtrum, and abnormal ears.

nodes.description: A subset of patients develops renal phosphate wasting with elevated FGF23, leading to hypophosphatemic rickets and severe bone demineralization.

evidence.reference_title: Hypophosphatemic rickets in opsismodysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Both children had hypophosphatemia, decreased TRP, and rickets.

evidence.reference_title: Hypophosphatemic rickets in opsismodysplasia.
evidence.snippet: We now demonstrate an association between opsismodysplasia, hypophosphatemic rickets, and FGF23 elevation.

nodes.description: Craniocervical abnormalities can include odontoid hypoplasia, creating potential concern for cervical instability.

evidence.reference_title: Opsismodysplasia: Phosphate Wasting Osteodystrophy Responds to Bisphosphonate Therapy.
evidence.snippet: Cervical spine films show odontoid hypoplasia, with no evidence of atlantoaxial or occipitocervical instability.

evidence.reference_title: Exome sequencing identifies INPPL1 mutations as a cause of opsismodysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Opsismodysplasia (OPS) is a severe autosomal-recessive chondrodysplasia characterized by pre- and postnatal micromelia with extremely short hands and feet.

evidence.reference_title: Whole-genome analysis reveals that mutations in inositol polyphosphate phosphatase-like 1 cause opsismodysplasia.
evidence.snippet: INPPL1 mutations explain ~60% of cases overall, including both of the families in our cohort with more than one affected child and 50% of the simplex cases.

evidence.reference_title: Prenatal-onset INPPL1-related skeletal dysplasia in two unrelated families: Diagnosis and prediction of lethality.
evidence.snippet: Schneckenbecken dysplasia and Opsismodysplasia are allelic disorders caused by pathogenic variants in INPPL1 gene with lethal outcomes in Schneckenbecken dysplasia and nonlethal prognosis in Opsismodysplasia.

evidence.reference_title: Prenatal-onset INPPL1-related skeletal dysplasia in two unrelated families: Diagnosis and prediction of lethality.
evidence.snippet: Prediction of lethality is difficult to determine in prenatal‐onset skeletal dysplasia especially with borderline lethality indices measured in prenatal ultrasound.

nodes.description: Loss of SHIP2 activity leads to elevated MEK-Erk1/2 signaling in chondrocytes, resulting in altered chondrocyte differentiation and impaired matrix mineralization. Growth plate histology shows a widened hypertrophic zone with irregular provisional calcification and disrupted vascular invasion. These defects impair endochondral ossification, the process by which cartilage is replaced by bone during skeletal development.
nodes.id: Impaired chondrocyte differentiation and endochondral ossification

evidence.reference_title: Altered chondrocyte differentiation, matrix mineralization and MEK-Erk1/2 signaling in an INPPL1 catalytic knock-out mouse model of opsismodysplasia.
evidence.snippet: levels of p-MEK and p-Erk1/2 were significantly increased in SHIP2-inactivated chondrocytes in response to serum and IGF-1, but not to FGF2, as compared to control chondrocytes.

evidence.reference_title: Altered chondrocyte differentiation, matrix mineralization and MEK-Erk1/2 signaling in an INPPL1 catalytic knock-out mouse model of opsismodysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Treatment of chondrocytes and bones in culture with a MEK inhibitor partially rescued the production of mineralized nodules, the size of the hypertrophic chondrocyte zone and bone growth, raising the possibility of a treatment that could partially reduce the phenotype of this severe condition.

evidence.reference_title: Opsismodysplasia: a new type of chondrodysplasia with predominant involvement of the bones of the hand and the vertebrae.
evidence.snippet: The growth cartilage studied in one case showed a wide hypertrophic area containing thick connective tissue septa, irregular provisional calcification, and vascular invasion.

evidence.reference_title: Exome sequencing identifies INPPL1 mutations as a cause of opsismodysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Our finding of INPPL1 mutations in OPS, a severe spondylodysplastic dysplasia with major growth plate disorganization, supports a key and specific role of this enzyme in endochondral ossification.

nodes.description: Delayed closure and persistent widening of the anterior fontanelle have been reported in surviving children.

evidence.reference_title: Opsismodysplasia resulting from an insertion mutation in the SH2 domain, which destabilizes INPPL1.
evidence.snippet: She had wide open anterior fontanelles which closed at six years of age.

nodes.description: Increased distance between the nasal base and upper lip.

evidence.reference_title: A Case of Opsismodysplasia with a Novel INPPL1 Variant.
evidence.snippet: He had dysmorphic findings including relative macrocephaly, midface hypoplasia, depressed nasal bridge, anteverted nostrils, long philtrum, small hands and feet, and brachydactyly.

nodes.description: INPPL1 encodes SHIP2, a phosphoinositide 5-phosphatase that dephosphorylates PI(3,4,5)P3 to generate PI(3,4)P2. Loss-of-function mutations in INPPL1 abolish this catalytic activity, disrupting both PI(3,4,5)P3 turnover and PI(3,4)P2 product-dependent phosphoinositide signaling in chondrocytes. Most pathogenic variants are premature stop codons or missense mutations affecting the catalytic domain, while some destabilize the protein through structural disruption of the SH2 domain.
nodes.id: Loss of SHIP2 phosphoinositide phosphatase activity

evidence.reference_title: Exome sequencing identifies INPPL1 mutations as a cause of opsismodysplasia.
evidence.snippet: INPPL1 belongs to the inositol-1,4,5-trisphosphate 5-phosphatase family, a family of signal-modulating enzymes that govern a plethora of cellular functions by regulating the levels of specific phosphoinositides.

evidence.reference_title: INPPL1 gene mutations in opsismodysplasia.
evidence.snippet: skeletal disease seems to result from the loss of SHIP2 activity due to catalytic domain mutations or no SHIP2 protein due to premature stop codons.

evidence.reference_title: Opsismodysplasia resulting from an insertion mutation in the SH2 domain, which destabilizes INPPL1.
evidence.snippet: the INPPL1 protein was barely detectable in patient cells.

evidence.reference_title: Novel compound heterozygous mutations in inositol polyphosphate phosphatase-like 1 in a family with severe opsismodysplasia.
evidence.snippet: The absence of SHIP2 was confirmed by immunoblot analysis of proband amniocytes.

nodes.description: Concave, cupped metaphyses of the long bones and tubular bones of the hands and feet are characteristic radiographic findings.

evidence.reference_title: Exome sequencing identifies INPPL1 mutations as a cause of opsismodysplasia.
evidence.snippet: The main radiological features are severe platyspondyly, squared metacarpals, delayed skeletal ossification, and metaphyseal cupping.

evidence.snippet: Opsismodysplasia (OPS) is a severe autosomal-recessive chondrodysplasia characterized by pre- and postnatal micromelia with extremely short hands and feet.

nodes.description: Delayed motor development has been reported in some survivors, likely reflecting the severity of the skeletal disease.

evidence.reference_title: Opsismodysplasia resulting from an insertion mutation in the SH2 domain, which destabilizes INPPL1.
evidence.snippet: She achieved neuromotor developmental milestones late.

nodes.description: A small thorax is a recurrent prenatal and postnatal finding and likely contributes to respiratory compromise in severe cases.

evidence.reference_title: Prenatal-onset INPPL1-related skeletal dysplasia in two unrelated families: Diagnosis and prediction of lethality.
evidence.snippet: Chest appears narrow and abdomen is protuberant.

nodes.description: Decreased bone mineralization, ranging from osteopenia to severe demineralization, particularly in patients with phosphate wasting.

evidence.snippet: Renal phosphate wasting is associated with severe bone demineralization and a more severe phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Pamidronate Treatment of a Patient with Opsismodysplasia and a Novel INPPL1 Variant: Efficacy, Mechanism, and Clinical Outcomes.
evidence.snippet: Initial evaluations showed severe short stature and low bone mineral density (DEXA SDS: -3.16).

nodes.description: Head circumference is relatively large compared to the shortened trunk and limbs, contributing to the characteristic craniofacial appearance.

evidence.snippet: He had dysmorphic findings including relative macrocephaly, midface hypoplasia, depressed nasal bridge, anteverted nostrils, long philtrum, small hands and feet, and brachydactyly.

evidence.reference_title: Whole-genome analysis reveals that mutations in inositol polyphosphate phosphatase-like 1 cause opsismodysplasia.
evidence.snippet: Opsismodysplasia is a rare, autosomal-recessive skeletal dysplasia characterized by short stature, characteristic facial features, and in some cases severe renal phosphate wasting.

nodes.description: Respiratory compromise ranges from neonatal distress to progressive respiratory failure in more severe cases.

evidence.reference_title: Opsismodysplasia: Phosphate Wasting Osteodystrophy Responds to Bisphosphonate Therapy.
evidence.snippet: These cases provide evidence of the variability in severity of findings in siblings with opsismodysplasia and suggest that those with phosphate wasting have more severe skeletal findings and respiratory compromise.

nodes.description: Spinal curvature can develop in surviving children.

evidence.snippet: She was identified to have scoliosis with a 42° right thoracic curve, and a 57° left lumbar curve.
